member.office_address: 1034 Longworth House Office Building Washington DC 20515-0547

form_analysis.form_instructions_text: Please use this form to submit your programmatic, report language, and bill language requests to the Office of Congressman Dave Min (CA-47) for the Fiscal Year 2027 appropriations bills. If you have multiple requests, please submit a separate form for each request. Please note the priority deadline is Friday, March 6th. After that, the office cannot guarantee that new submissions will be reviewed. If you have any questions, need to make amendments to your submission, or need to submit supporting documents, please email ca47appropriations@mail.house.gov.

sections.description: Information about the requesting organization and the primary point of contact.
General details regarding the specific appropriations bill and agency targeted by this request.

fields.label: Appropriations Bill
options: Agriculture, Rural Development, Food and Drug Administration, and Related Agencies
Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies
Energy and Water Development and Related Agencies
Labor, Health and Human Services, Education, and Related Agencies
Military Construction, Veterans' Affairs, and Related Agencies
National Security, Department of State, and Related Programs
Transportation, Housing and Urban Development, and Related Agencies
fields.draft_value: Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies
fields.rationale: NASA Science Mission Directorate funding is provided through the Commerce, Justice, Science, and Related Agencies Appropriations bill.

fields.label: Agency / Department
fields.rationale: Directly matches the agency specified in the reference values for the Science Mission Directorate.

fields.label: Request Type
fields.help_text: Indicate if this is a programmatic, report language, or bill language request.
fields.rationale: This is a request for a specific funding level ($9 billion) for an existing agency program (NASA SMD), which is categorized as a programmatic request.

sections.description: Details regarding requested funding amounts and historical funding levels.

fields.label: Requested Program
fields.rationale: Matches the specific account name provided in the reference values.

fields.label: Requested Amount
fields.rationale: This is the exact funding ask amount provided in the reference values for the Science Mission Directorate.

fields.label: Amount Included in the President's Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Request
fields.rationale: The reference values explicitly state that the FY2027 President's Budget Request has not yet been released and is currently unknown.

fields.label: Previously Enacted Amount
fields.help_text: Provide the enacted dollar amount from the most recent prior fiscal year.
fields.rationale: This value matches the pre-filled value and the FY2026 enacted total provided in the reference values.

fields.label: Purpose, Impact, and Justification of Request
fields.draft_value:
* Restores NASA Science to $9B, supporting $17.2M in recent district investments across 59 grants to UC Irvine and 17 contracts to local aerospace firms.
* Sustains UC Irvine's critical work on Antarctic ice sheet mapping and atmospheric gas sampling, projects that rely on stable, multi-year NASA Science funding.
* Funds high-tech aerospace jobs at Iceye US ($2.2M for radar mapping) and Tyvak Nano-Satellite Systems ($1.5M), reinforcing CA-47 as a center for satellite innovation.
* Secures U.S. leadership in the space race against China while providing a proven 3:1 economic return on every dollar invested in NASA Science.
fields.rationale: High confidence. Value directly references the $9B programmatic ask and matches it to $17.2M in CA-47 spending data. Specific recipients (UC Irvine, Iceye US, Tyvak) and research projects (ice sheet mapping) are cited from the provided context. Incorporates strategic points on ROI and competition.

sections.description: Details for report or bill language requests.

fields.label: Title of Language Request
fields.draft_value: NASA Science Mission Directorate Funding Request
fields.rationale: The Request Context indicates that no bill or report language is being requested for this programmatic funding request.

fields.label: Text of Language Request
fields.rationale: Low confidence. This field belongs to a language request section. The request context states this is a programmatic request and no bill or report language is being requested (N/A).

fields.label: Included in a Prior Bill
fields.rationale: The NASA Science Mission Directorate is an existing program that received $7.25 billion in the FY2026 enacted bill (H.R. 6938).

fields.label: Purpose, Impact, and Justification of Request
fields.rationale: Low confidence. This field appears to be the justification for a language request. As this is a programmatic request with no language text (N/A), this field is not applicable.

top_grants.description: For atmospheric chemistry  one of the grand challenges is to build a quantitative  predictive understanding of the budgets of tropospheric Ozone (O3) and methane (CH4). In terms of managing climate change and air quality over this century we need chemistry-climate models (CCMS) with solid predictive capability. We propose to develop a set of observation-based  process-oriented metrics to test ccms on their ability to match conditions related to the o3 and ch4 budgets. The core data for these metrics will come from the NASA dc8 aircraft in situ measurements from several recent campaigns. The major task is to build an observational database of chemical statistics  including high-order products and chemical reaction rates that relate to these budgets. Combined with model diagnostics  these become metrics that can test and differentiate across the spectrum of ccms so as to identify good performers and outliers. These metrics will be developed and tested with community input and will be ready for the planning of the next major cycle of climate assessments  which have now become a standard cycle for atmospheric chemistry models. The overall goal here is to provide metrics for guidance in model development and testing  in order to document improvements in our atmospheric chemistry models regarding the budgets of o3 and ch4 and other photo-oxidized species. The primary data sets used here are from aircraft in situ measurements that give a near-complete measurement of atmospheric composition on a 10-second flight path. As we have shown for the atmospheric tomography (ATOM) mission  such data allow us to calculate the daily budgets of o3  ch4 and other photo-oxidized species such as co or dimethyl sulfide. We will combine the atom data (representing the vast remote-from-pollution atmosphere) with the korus-aq and firex-aq data (representing the immediate pollution-source atmosphere)  thus providing us a view of the ranges in photochemistry. We plan to develop metrics for ccms that examine the production and loss of o3  following from near source to remote Troposphere  and identifying the specific nature of chemistry that drives it. The other critical component of the o3 budget is the Stratosphere to Troposphere Exchange (STE) flux. While we can easily 'see' ste folding events  it is nigh impossible to integrate the net o3 flux with any degree of certainty. Instead  our strategy uses a mix of o3 and n2o measurements from aura mls and omi  scisat/ace  and noaa surface measurements of n2o. These metrics are effective proxies for the atmospheric chemistry and transport processes related to ste  and good model performance for these gives confidence in the model derived o3 flux. This research is clearly tied to several of the atmospheric composition focus area science questions: it documents and helps explain changing composition it quantifies the stratospheric influx of Ozone and possible surface air quality effects it builds confidence in model projections of lifetimes and fluxes in the future. In terms of the accdam call  it combines aircraft data sets in new ways and extends their use evaluating chemistry-climate models. In terms of the overall Earth science research program  it provides a new basis for evaluating NASA chemistry models and the merra-2 meteorological fields.
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Validating climate models predicting tropospheric O3 and CH4 budgets.

Top Active Awards.Description: For atmospheric chemistry  one of the grand challenges is to build a quantitative  predictive understanding of the budgets of tropospheric Ozone (O3) and methane (CH4). In terms of managing climate change and air quality over this century we need chemistry-climate models (CCMS) with solid predictive capability. We propose to develop a set of observation-based  process-oriented metrics to test ccms on their ability to match conditions related to the o3 and ch4 budgets. The core data for these metrics will come from the NASA dc8 aircraft in situ measurements from several recent campaigns. The major task is to build an observational database of chemical statistics  including high-order products and chemical reaction rates that relate to these budgets. Combined with model diagnostics  these become metrics that can test and differentiate across the spectrum of ccms so as to identify good performers and outliers. These metrics will be developed and tested with community input and will be ready for the planning of the next major cycle of climate assessments  which have now become a standard cycle for atmospheric chemistry models. The overall goal here is to provide metrics for guidance in model development and testing  in order to document improvements in our atmospheric chemistry models regarding the budgets of o3 and ch4 and other photo-oxidized species. The primary data sets used here are from aircraft in situ measurements that give a near-complete measurement of atmospheric composition on a 10-second flight path. We plan to develop metrics for ccms that examine the production and loss of o3  following from near source to remote Troposphere  and identifying the specific nature of chemistry that drives it. The other critical component of the o3 budget is the Stratosphere to Troposphere Exchange (STE) flux. Instead  our strategy uses a mix of o3 and n2o measurements from aura mls and omi  scisat/ace  and noaa surface measurements of n2o. These metrics are effective proxies for the atmospheric chemistry and transport processes related to ste  and good model performance for these gives confidence in the model derived o3 flux. This research is clearly tied to several of the atmospheric composition focus area science questions: it documents and helps explain changing composition it quantifies the stratospheric influx of Ozone and possible surface air quality effects it builds confidence in model projections of lifetimes and fluxes in the future. In terms of the accdam call  it combines aircraft data sets in new ways and extends their use evaluating chemistry-climate models. In terms of the overall Earth science research program  it provides a new basis for evaluating NASA chemistry models and the merra-2 meteorological fields.
